speaker: interviewer
text: And his family. Mike, unfortunately your whole family isn't here with us right now. But could you introduce those of us who are here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is just a small portion of the family. And this is Esther, my wife, and I met her in Israel. This is my oldest daughter, Frida. She was born in Israel. And this is one of my grandchildren, Josh, who was born in the United States. And hopefully we'll be a big family like we were back in Czechoslovakia that I lost 49 members.

speaker: interviewer
text: Josh, how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm eight right now, but I'm turning nine in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What grade are you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Third.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what it means for your grandfather to be a survivor of the Holocaust? Are you studying about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I like to read about it and stuff. I like-- like in newspapers and books. And I like to see movies and stuff. And I'm not-- I'm not studying about it in class, though we did have an assembly, on what's called Yom HaShoah in-- in Is-- in Hebrew. And my Saba probably-- well, I call him Saba-- he probably feels pretty lucky that he didn't die.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're very fortunate to have him. Frida, how long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was four when we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you always been aware of the fact that you're a child of a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way has it affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It sort of has a dual effect. It makes one optimistic, but it also is a constant reminder that things can be taken away from-- from you [BACKGROUND NOISE] in a flash.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think the rest of the family just arrived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mike, we just had a knock on the door, so we have a new arrival. Could you introduce your other daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my middle daughter, Anita.

speaker: interviewer
text: Welcome. I was just asking Frida if she was aware of the fact that she was the child of a Holocaust survivor. Have you felt that difference in your life? Have you been aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a lot of different ways. In family matters, in social, outside of the home, as well as inside the home. Mostly felt a difference with the people outside of the home. I don't know that they really feel what we can feel. They can empathize and sympathize, but I don't know that they really have an understanding like we do, being the children of a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Esther, do you think people have understood your husband's experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of them, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it's possible to even understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think it's possible to understand the pain and the sorrow, and the tragedy they have been through. No. It's not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your-- is your family-- did you-- where were you during the time of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: '34. So-- so you are also a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a survivor, but not exactly a Holocaust survivor, because I wasn't in the concentration camps. But I survived a very hard time in Russia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- but your family survived-- your-- your Jewish family survived those years--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Europe, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother is-- she came from a family of 14 kids. She's the-- two of them survived, one in Mexico City and my mother in Russia, from 14. My father had a sister with two children. They perished in the Holocaust, so he survived. So just a very small part of the family actually survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it in Russia that you lived during the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent. With your mother and father and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was born there. He was seven days old when we left Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frida, I know that you need to leave. Is there anything that you would like to add before you go? I do have a couple more questions for your father that I would like to ask. But if there's anything you'd like to add--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to thank my father for giving us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the optimism we need to go through things that life poses in front of us. And I'd like to tell him that we think he is terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's beautifully put.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can Anita and I trade places?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Esther, when were you married to Mike in Israel?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: '52, Right? I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that survivors were well received in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. Maybe I was too young to realize it. But one thing I do know, that I was privileged to be married to Mike. And I thank him for all those years-- great father, excellent husband, and a big supporter in every way in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's given us everything he didn't get.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a-- my best friend and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we wish you many more years like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, was it an effort to give your family everything you didn't have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to have everything. I mean, I want them to have everything that I wasn't able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your profession in Israel? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an oil well dealer-- very unusual for Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we didn't find oil, but we found water, so it was just as good.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we drill for oil in Israel. As a matter of fact, I was sent to France [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to work for French companies to drill for oil in France in 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was life in Israel difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we didn't know nothing else. So I think it was-- wasn't easy. We worked hard. We worked 12 hours a day, but it was normal. In those days it was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really didn't decide to come. It's-- like I said, I was sent to France. I was working in France for an Israeli company. And after I saw that there is other possibilities-- not to, especially, to leave Israel, just to go, and to work, and to-- to know the world. I know that one day I'm going to go back to Israel. I decided to stay in France. And I didn't return to Israel. I stayed in France for a while, and then we decided to go to Venezuela, because I was told that there is-- they pay very well to oil drill out there. It was a stupid decision, but that's what I did. While we arranged the papers, a civil war broke in Venezuela. So we still decided to go somewhere, and we went to-- to Mexico. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to a place that add to my chances to do anything. But Esther had her mother there, so we decided to go to Mexico.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Anita was-- and Anita was born there. And Anita was born there. So we were waiting a short while until she grows up, and then we decided to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anita, what year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, you heard me ask your two daughters about whether you being a Holocaust survivor has affected them. Do you think that it's affected them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they were little, maybe they [INAUDIBLE] that I was a different and difficult person because of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very honest for you to admit that. In what ways do you think you were difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was nervous, but she helped me to get out of it. And when you're nervous without any reason, then the children, of course, they feel it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I think, with all these problems, I'm a good person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mind if I ask your wife and your daughter about how--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --What that was like, living with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: --When you were nervous. Was it nervous or anxious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nervous, anxious, everything that you can imagine that a little boy went through, and a person that thinks different, sees things differently than people don't that are not survivors. Many things that it's very hard to probably even to explain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because there is no one day-- I am a very happy person. My life-- I like nice things and I like jokes and I'm a happy person and we're a happy family. But there was no day in my life-- I could have been in Club Med, or in a boat, or wherever--

speaker: interviewee
text: That you didn't think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I was not thinking about it. There's certain difficult things that are just very hard not to remember, day by day, for a half a second, for without any reason. For 20 years, I was-- if I was fighting someone, at night, I was dreaming about him as a German solder. The German helmet and the German boot was-- were part of my life. I was always thinking-- a mean person, he was a German to me when I was dreaming. And this was very hard to-- to fight, and Esther helped me to get over these battles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have those thought associations? I mean, random associations where you'll see something and immediately you'll return to that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. It will be always. And whenever I have chance, I'm visiting Auschwitz, and Mauthausen, and Dachau. And sometimes I think it helps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it important for us to remember those of us who were not there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. Very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Esther, what was it like to live with Mike when he was going through that difficult stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was important to understand. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What helped you to understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a few professionals to ask how to deal with it. And it wasn't so bad, because he's very good and he always fought and everything. And the only thing I tried is to bring him that he should be able to talk about it. Because the first years, he couldn't. And once he got to the point when he started to talk, he really became [INAUDIBLE]. He became easier and more relaxed. He faced the fact that they are gone. It took him a very long time. He used to write letters to everybody in the world. He was searching for them. And once we went back for the first time to Dachau and he faced it, and he cried for the first time in his life after the [INAUDIBLE]-- the [INAUDIBLE] liberated. It became easier for him, and he became much more relaxed. And he faced his loss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anita, was-- did-- did your father talk to you about his experiences? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. He always made it-- he always tried, when the appropriate time arose, he-- he did want to talk about it. In fact, he encourages us to go visit, and I did go a few times already. And I think because we're living proof of what he's passed, he continues to, you know, educate us in that manner and hope that we will carry it on for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said appropriate time, do you mean when you were old enough, or do you mean when he was ready to talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was both. I think it's when he was ready to talk, as well as his children having a comprehension and an understanding-- a better-- yet better understanding. Each-- each period of what he had passed, he found the appropriate way to talk about it. If we could understand it, he would bring it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We really need the time for the pictures. I wish we could go on talking. And I actually-- I've been wanting to ask you one thing about your Bar Mitzvah in the ghetto, if you could just quickly tell us a little bit about that. And then we need to look at the pictures of your family, the pictures you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was over 50 years ago, but I remember it was a very poor Bar Mitzvah. But still, everybody was there. All my friends were there. The liquor was made at home, I remember, from some fruits. I don't know. In those days, people were very handy to do things. And there were cakes and everything. And I even remember that I had a pair of new shoes. They didn't last too long-- they were torn apart after three or four days, because they were manufactured during the war-- just terrible. But I had a new suit, and I had a new pair of shoes for the Bar Mitzvah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- did a lot of the Jewish community come to celebrate with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think-- I think over there you bring-- yes, yes, not just family. Friends and-- and relatives. I don't know how many-- how many were able to come there. But it was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become a man then, or do you think you became a man during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war. Then I was a little boy, just a very little boy who didn't know too much. Children thirteen years, then, were completely different than a thirteen-year-old. I didn't have the mind of my grandchild of eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, I want to thank you very much for an inspiring interview. We've learned a lot from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for everything you've shared. And thank you to Esther, and Anita, and your whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for having us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture is my mother when she was approximately 17 years old. Her name is Frida Ackerman, and it was taken, I think, in 1922, four years after the first world war. If she was born in 1905, then she was 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Levice. It's a school picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Levice, Czechoslovakia, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levice, Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a school picture. And here is my brother, Andras Popik. He's eight years old, second grade. He's the third one from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the bottom row, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the bottom row. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most of these children, their teacher, Mr Miller. It was a Jewish school. Mr Miller emigrated to Israel, to Palestine, in 1938. So he probably died there. There are a few children that they are still living in Israel. Most of them died in the concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is the picture of Vivian and Jeffrey. Vivian was born in the United States, in Los Angeles, in 1964. And she's married to Jeff.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Los Angeles, taken in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture was taken in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Jeff Gaines. So it's Vivian and Jeff Gaines now, my youngest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And does she have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. She is a schoolteacher, and she couldn't be here. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is Frida, that she was here with her older son. Here he's very young. I think he's four years old. And this is Evan Glukoff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken, and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken also in Los Angeles, and it was taken nine years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we met Frida's other son, Josh, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met Frieda's other son, Josh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are-- are they her children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She has two boys. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter, Alexa. She was born a year ago, and she's the daughter of Anita and Benny Noff. Anita was here, and Ben is on the other picture. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Yeah, this is the picture of my daughter, Anita, that she was here, with her husband, Benny-- Benny Noff-- and my granddaughter, Alexa. She was two months old in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken 10 months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, in their house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my eldest daughter, Frida, that we saw her, and her husband, Joe Glukoff. The picture was taken about 10 years ago in their backyard in Los Angeles.
